dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 4 responding for abdominal pain and problems.
Hi, I'm Kyle, a paramedic with the rescue squad. What brought you to call 911 today?
I've been having severe abdominal pain since last night at 11 o'clock. It's a sharp pain, and it's been a 9 out of 10 all night.
Can you open your eyes for me?
Can you tell me the date, today's day, and where you are located?
Please squeeze both of my fingers to show me you can follow my instructions.
I see. Have you experienced anything else with the pain, like nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea?
Yes, I've been vomiting and have had diarrhea. I've also been feeling very nauseous.
Do you have any known allergies we should be aware of?
I have an allergy to Bactrim.
What medications are you currently taking?
I take Lasix, Wellbutrin, and antivirals for HIV.
Have you ever had any major medical conditions or surgeries?
I have HIV and had acute pancreatitis in the past.
Your breathing is not labored, but let's confirm airway patency as we proceed.
Your breath sounds are clear and equal bilaterally.
Radial pulse is strong and regular, 68 per minute. Skin is pink, warm, and dry.
Your pulse is 68 beats per minute with a regular rhythm and no arrhythmias.
You are breathing at a rate of 16 times per minute, unlabored and without distress.
Your blood pressure is 149 over 91.
SpO2 is 97% with a normal waveform.
Your blood glucose is 101 mg/dL.
I'll start 4-lead monitoring for a baseline ECG.
This is a 12-lead ECG being obtained now. The rhythm is normal sinus rhythm with no ST-segment or T-wave changes.
You are alert and oriented to time, person, and place. No signs of altered mental status.
Have you been experiencing any other symptoms like fever, cough, chest pain, or sweating?
No, I haven't had fever, cough, chest pain, or sweating.
There are no rashes, bruises, petechiae, or bite marks visible on your skin.
Pupils are equal, round, and reactive to light.
No nasal drainage, oral injuries, or throat issues found.
Bilateral breath sounds are clear, no adventitious sounds noted.
Tenderness is noted in the abdomen, but there is no distension or signs of guarding or rigidity.
No deformities, angulations, or burns on your limbs. Mobility appears intact.
I'll now attempt to establish an intravenous line in case we need to provide fluids or meds.
The first IV attempt at the extremity vein was unsuccessful.
The second IV attempt was also unsuccessful. We will not be moving forward with an IV for now.
We'll transport you to [COMPANY_NAME] for further evaluation and treatment.
Please stand up slowly; I'll assist you to the stretcher and secure you with a seatbelt.
You are now safely seated and secured with the seatbelt on the stretcher for transport.
We are preparing to depart now and will notify the hospital on our way.
Let me check your vitals during transport. Pulse is still 67, respiratory rate is 16, and oxygen saturation is 99%.
Blood pressure is now 130 over 86. No significant changes since initial assessment.
The symptom severity has not changed impact our initial transport destination.
Hospital ER, we have a 57-year-old male with a primary complaint of severe abdominal pain accompanied by nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea.
Initial vital signs were BP 149/91, pulse 68, and SpO2 97%. No fever, cough, chest pain, or sweating were detected.
The second check showed BP 130/86, pulse 67, and SpO2 99%. Pain severity remains at 9/10.
The 12-lead ECG presented as normal sinus rhythm with no ectopy or ST elevation.
No signs of respiratory distress. The patient's skin was pink, warm, and dry with good peripheral perfusion.
No Cullen's or Grey-Turner signs, and the patient remained alert and oriented throughout the assessment.
Mental status is A&Ox4 with a GCS of 15, no signs of altered mental status or confusion.
Medical history includes HIV and a previous episode of acute pancreatitis. No new conditions have emerged.
He is currently taking Lasix, Wellbutrin, and antivirals as medication, and there are no changes in his regimen.
He has an allergy to Bactrim, and no other existing or recent allergies have been reported.
There were no instances of cough, fever, sweating, chest pain, or shortness of breath during the entire assessment.
Continuous glucose was not obtained successfully, with a final reading of 0 mg/dL.
The patient was moved into the ambulance and seated safely with full ambulation ability.
A complete verbal report was given to the hospital staff en route.
The patient walked down from the ambulance and into the wheelchair in the ER upon arrival.
A full report was given to the receiving hospital, and care was officially turned over.
No further interventions were performed in the field. [UNIQUE_IDENTIFIER] is returning to service now.
